findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` to the Files list and explicitly include `caused_by` in the `check_would_create_cycle` non-cycle allowlist (line 204), mirroring the `discovered_from` entry.
evidence: Plan Approach: 'non-blocking, non-cycle like discovered_from'
Plan Files: `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` is absent from the listed files
_graph.py:204: `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"): return False` — `caused_by` is not in this tuple, falls to else-branch executing block-cycle semantics
findings.finding: `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` is missing from the Files list, but `check_would_create_cycle` has a hardcoded allowlist of non-cycle relations (`if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"): return False`). Without adding `caused_by` to this list, a `caused_by` link will fall through to the `else` branch (blocks semantics), run `_get_all_blocked_by(target_id)`, and potentially raise a spurious `CyclicDependencyError` for any `caused_by` pair that shares a path with blocking edges — contradicting the plan's stated 'non-cycle-inducing' property.
scenarios: User runs `rebar link bug-A change-B caused_by` where bug-A transitively blocks change-B via `blocks` edges; `_get_all_blocked_by(change-B)` returns bug-A, `check_would_create_cycle` returns True, and `add_dependency` raises CyclicDependencyError — rejecting a valid causal link.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_graph.py`, change `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"):` to `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"):` and add `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` to the Files list in the plan.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/_commands/unlink.py` and `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt` to the Files list and update their hardcoded relation strings to include `caused_by`.
evidence: Plan Files: lists `src/rebar/_cli/help/link.txt` but not `unlink.py` or `overview.txt`
unlink.py:28: `"  relation: blocks | depends_on | relates_to | duplicates | supersedes | discovered_from\n"`
overview.txt:16: `"link        Link two tickets (relation REQUIRED: blocks|depends_on|relates_to|duplicates|supersedes|discovered_from)"`
findings.finding: Several hardcoded relation-list strings that enumerate the full relation vocabulary are not covered by the plan's Files list: `src/rebar/_commands/unlink.py` (usage string, line 28-30) and `src/rebar/_cli/help/overview.txt` (line 16). These will display a stale 6-value relation list to users after `caused_by` is added, creating a user-visible documentation inconsistency.
scenarios: `rebar link --help` (overview) still shows only 6 relations; `rebar unlink --help` usage still shows 6 relations — users cannot discover `caused_by` from CLI help.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` to the Files section and update the hardcoded non-blocking-relations tuple at line 204 to include `caused_by` (or, better, derive it as `CANONICAL_RELATIONS - _BLOCKING_RELATIONS` to prevent future drift).
evidence: Plan Files section lists `src/rebar/graph/_links.py` (CANONICAL_RELATIONS) but NOT `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py`.
Codebase: `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py:204`: `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from'): return False` — hardcoded inline tuple, not derived from CANONICAL_RELATIONS.

Plan Approach: 'Follow the discovered_from precedent exactly (directional, non-blocking, non-cycle…)' — but non-cycle behaviour is gated by the inline tuple in _graph.py, not CANONICAL_RELATIONS alone.
The same pattern applies to `_links.py:225-226` comment reference and `_hierarchy.py:181`, but _graph.py:204 is load-bearing for cycle correctness.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` (line 204) contains a hardcoded inline tuple of non-blocking relations — `('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from')` — rather than deriving the set from `CANONICAL_RELATIONS - _BLOCKING_RELATIONS`. This is a pre-existing NIH micro-pattern (an inline copy that can diverge from the authoritative frozenset). The plan omits `_graph.py` from the Files section, so when `caused_by` is added to `CANONICAL_RELATIONS` only, `check_would_create_cycle` will NOT treat `caused_by` as non-cycle-inducing: the else-branch will run cycle detection on it, contradicting the plan's stated 'non-cycle like discovered_from' property.
scenarios: A user runs `rebar link bug-A bug-B caused_by`; `check_would_create_cycle` enters the else branch (not the early-return False branch) and performs BFS cycle detection — possibly raising a false CyclicDependencyError when a reverse link exists, or silently succeeding but with wrong semantics.
The plan's AC1 test round-trips the link, but a cycle-direction test (e.g., linking A→B→A via caused_by) would expose the missed update.
findings.suggested_fix: In `_graph.py:204`, change `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from'):` to `if relation not in _BLOCKING_RELATIONS:` (importing `_BLOCKING_RELATIONS` from `._relations`), making the check self-maintaining as new non-blocking relations are added.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` to the Files list and update `check_would_create_cycle` (line 204) to include `caused_by` in the hardcoded non-blocking exemption tuple (alongside `relates_to`, `duplicates`, `supersedes`, `discovered_from`).
evidence: Plan states: 'non-blocking, non-cycle like discovered_from'
_graph.py:204: `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"): return False` — hardcoded tuple, not CANONICAL_RELATIONS-driven
Plan's Files list does not include src/rebar/graph/_graph.py
Without editing this line, a caused_by self-link or cross-link would enter the blocking-cycle BFS, contradicting the plan's guarantee
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'non-cycle like discovered_from' but _graph.py:204 has a HARDCODED tuple — not driven by CANONICAL_RELATIONS — that explicitly exempts named relations from cycle detection. `caused_by` is absent from this tuple, so check_would_create_cycle will fall through to the blocking-relation branch and incorrectly treat caused_by as cycle-capable. The plan names only _links.py under Files; _graph.py is omitted.
scenarios: rebar link A B caused_by followed by rebar link B A caused_by raises CyclicDependencyError unexpectedly, since caused_by is not in the non-blocking exemption set
AC test 'a caused_by self-cycle does not raise' would FAIL because check_would_create_cycle treats caused_by as blocking
findings.suggested_fix: Change _graph.py:204 from `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"):` to `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"):`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `caused_by` to the `check_would_create_cycle` allowlist in `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` line 204 so it short-circuits as non-cycle-inducing, consistent with all other non-blocking relations.
evidence: Plan names graph/_links.py as the target but not graph/_graph.py
check_would_create_cycle at line 204: `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"): return False` — caused_by is absent
The function's docstring at line 189 explicitly says 'relates_to, duplicates, supersedes, and discovered_from never create cycles'
findings.finding: src/rebar/graph/_graph.py line 204 contains an explicit allowlist `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from'): return False` in `check_would_create_cycle`. Without adding `caused_by` to this allowlist, a `caused_by` link would fall through to the `blocks` cycle-check branch, incorrectly applying a cycle guard to a non-blocking relation.
scenarios: A user runs `rebar link a b caused_by` — the graph checks whether it creates a cycle using the blocks-path logic, potentially raising a false CyclicDependencyError or silently permitting an incorrect check.
findings.suggested_fix: Change `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from"):` to `if relation in ("relates_to", "duplicates", "supersedes", "discovered_from", "caused_by"):`

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add `_graph.py` to the ## Files list and specify that `caused_by` must be added to the `check_would_create_cycle` non-blocking allowlist (line 204) so the relation short-circuits to `False` before the BFS runs.
evidence: Hardcoded allowlist at _graph.py:204: `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from'): return False`
Plan's ## Files section lists files to update but does not mention `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py`
Plan AC: 'a `caused_by` self-cycle does not raise' — this is the exact scenario where the missing allowlist entry bites
CANONICAL_RELATIONS in _links.py and the inline tuple in _graph.py are separate, independent data structures with no shared derivation
findings.finding: `_graph.py:check_would_create_cycle` contains a hardcoded inline tuple `('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from')` at line 204 that governs which relations bypass cycle-detection. The plan lists files to update but omits `_graph.py`, so `caused_by` will NOT be in this allowlist after the change. Consequently `check_would_create_cycle('a','a','caused_by',...)` will NOT short-circuit to `False` and instead runs the `blocks`-path BFS, causing the plan's own AC ('a `caused_by` self-cycle does not raise') to FAIL.
scenarios: `rebar link a a caused_by` calls `check_would_create_cycle('a','a','caused_by',...)`, falls through to the `blocks` BFS path, which returns True for a self-link, causing `CyclicDependencyError` — contradicting the plan's own AC.
Any `caused_by` link between two tickets that share a transitive `blocks` path could incorrectly raise `CyclicDependencyError`.
findings.suggested_fix: In `src/rebar/graph/_graph.py` line 204, change `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from'):` to `if relation in ('relates_to', 'duplicates', 'supersedes', 'discovered_from', 'caused_by'):`. Alternatively, derive this set from `CANONICAL_RELATIONS - _BLOCKING_RELATIONS` to make future additions automatically correct.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC verifying at least one documentation file is updated (e.g., `docs/cli-reference.md` or `link.txt` contains 'caused_by') and a separate AC (or a test assertion) confirming `outbound_links.py` does NOT map `caused_by` to any Jira link type.
evidence: Files section lists: 'src/rebar/_cli/help/link.txt, src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py, docs/ticket-model.md, docs/event-schema.md, docs/cli-reference.md — document it'
Files section lists: 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_links.py — leave caused_by intentionally Jira-unmapped'
ABSENCE: No AC references any of these six files or verifies their content was updated
findings.finding: Five documentation/auxiliary deliverables named in the Files section (`src/rebar/_cli/help/link.txt`, `src/rebar/_mcp_writes.py`, `docs/ticket-model.md`, `docs/event-schema.md`, `docs/cli-reference.md`) and one implementation file (`src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_links.py`) have no covering Acceptance Criterion, so they can be silently skipped without failing any AC.
scenarios: An implementer ships the schema+type+graph changes but skips all documentation files; all three ACs still pass.
The Jira-unmapping in outbound_links.py is accidentally wired up; no AC would catch the regression.
findings.suggested_fix: Add: '- [ ] `caused_by` does not appear in the Jira outbound link map — assert `caused_by not in JIRA_LINK_TYPE_MAP` (or equivalent) in the unit test file. `docs/cli-reference.md` (or `link.txt`) contains the string `caused_by` — verified by a grep in CI or a doc-smoke test.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Strengthen the AC1 jq proving command to assert non-empty cardinality, e.g. pipe through `| length` and assert > 0, or use `jq -e 'first(.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")) // error'` so the command exits non-zero when the relation is absent.
evidence: AC1 text: `rebar show <a> --output json | jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")'`

VERIFY-COMMAND DEFECT TAXONOMY #5 (missing cardinality assertion) and #4 (fixture invalidity risk): `jq -e` with a select that emits no output exits 0 in jq ≥1.6 when there are zero results from a streaming expression, making the AC passable even if `caused_by` is absent from the links array.
findings.finding: The jq proving command in AC1 uses `select(.relation=="caused_by")` which can silently exit 0 with no output when the relation is absent, depending on jq version — a substring false-positive / missing cardinality assertion defect that can produce a green result even when the link was never stored.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1: `rebar show <a> --output json | jq -e '.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")'`
scenarios: The link was never written to the store but the command exits 0 because the empty-stream case is not caught.
A future refactor renames the field and the AC still passes silently.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the proving command with:
```
rebar show <a> --output json | jq -e '[.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")] | length > 0'
```
This exits 1 (failure) when the select produces no results, making the AC actually falsifiable.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the rollback description to cover already-persisted `caused_by` events: either document that the reducer must tolerate unknown relation values (ignore-unknown strategy) when rolling back, or confirm the schema validator is lenient enough that old events remain replayable after the enum value is removed.
evidence: Approach: 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'
Approach: 'no persisted data is rewritten'
ABSENCE: No mention of what happens to already-persisted caused_by events when the schema is reverted (new events in old schema = validation failure on replay)
findings.finding: The stated rollback path ('back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value') is incomplete: once `caused_by` LINK events are persisted in the store, rolling back the code removes the schema enum value, causing the reducer/validator to reject the already-stored events on replay. The plan does not address this forward-data / backward-code scenario.
scenarios: Team ships caused_by support, users create caused_by links, team must roll back; replaying the event log against the rolled-back schema fails validation on every caused_by LINK event, breaking the store.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach: 'The reducer MUST tolerate unknown/unrecognized relation values (pass-through or skip) so that a rollback to a schema without `caused_by` does not break replay of existing events. AC3 back-compat test should also verify an event with relation `caused_by` is handled gracefully by a hypothetical reducer that does not know the value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assert the post-rollback schema state for compacted SNAPSHOTs containing `caused_by` deps: either (a) document that the reducer tolerates unknown relation values in `compiled_state.deps` and no schema validation is enforced on replay, or (b) add a compensating-forward strip step that rewrites unknown relations to `relates_to` (or similar) before the next compaction, with a `DATA LOSS RISK` note if (b) is chosen.
evidence: Plan: 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'
ticket-compact.sh bakes LINK events into a SNAPSHOT compiled_state.deps[] and deletes original *-LINK.json files (graph/_links.py lines 81–103)

If the `relation` enum in common.schema.json is reverted to 6 values, any SNAPSHOT containing `caused_by` in deps[].relation will violate the schema or silently produce an unvalidated relation in the reducer
The back-compat AC test fixture covers only 'a pre-existing LINK event with a legacy relation' (forward direction), not the reverse: a compacted SNAPSHOT with `caused_by` deps read after rollback

ABSENCE: plan has no assertion that the reducer tolerates unknown relation values in compiled_state.deps post-rollback, nor a compensating-forward strategy (e.g. strip unknown relations on SNAPSHOT restore)
findings.finding: The plan's rollback path ('simply ceasing to emit the value') does not assert the post-rollback schema state for tickets that were compacted (via ticket-compact.sh) while `caused_by` links were active: those SNAPSHOTs have `caused_by` baked into `compiled_state.deps[].relation`, and the plan does not specify whether schema validation after enum rollback will reject them or whether the reducer is guaranteed to tolerate an unrecognised relation value.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'back-out is simply ceasing to emit the value'; ## Acceptance Criteria — back-compat test covers legacy relation forward only
scenarios: Engineer ships `caused_by`, teams create and compact tickets with caused_by links, then rolls back by reverting the enum — compacted SNAPSHOTs now contain an enum value not in the schema, breaking schema validation for those tickets.
After rollback, `rebar show <ticket>` on a compacted ticket with a caused_by dep silently drops or errors on the relation depending on reducer behaviour, which is unspecified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or sub-assertion in the existing unit test) verifying that `rebar show <b> --output json` does NOT contain a `caused_by` edge pointing back to `<a>` after `rebar link <a> <b> caused_by`, confirming the directional invariant.
evidence: Plan states 'directional, non-blocking, non-cycle-inducing' in the What section and Approach section
AC1 only asserts `show <a>` contains the edge; no AC asserts `show <b>` lacks a reverse edge
Absence of any reverse-direction assertion in the Tests bullet list
findings.finding: The plan claims `caused_by` is directional but no acceptance criterion or test verifies that the reverse direction is NOT created — i.e., that `show <b> --output json` does NOT show a `caused_by` edge back to `<a>`. If the relation is accidentally implemented symmetrically, all written ACs still pass.
scenarios: Developer accidentally mirrors the link in both directions (as some relation types do); all three ACs pass green; graph traversal for 'source of bugs' returns wrong results at runtime
findings.suggested_fix: Extend `test_caused_by_relation.py` with: after `link a b caused_by`, assert that `show b --output json | jq '[.links[]|select(.relation=="caused_by")]|length'` equals 0 (or that only the forward edge from `a` exists).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief design-decision note (ADR entry, AGENTS.md section, or a dedicated heading in docs/ticket-model.md) capturing why `caused_by` is a distinct relation from `discovered_from` (causal vs. temporal semantics) and the general policy for adding new non-blocking, Jira-unmapped relations.
evidence: Plan states: 'A dedicated relation (not reusing discovered_from, whose semantics are temporal not causal) is required so recovery-time and source-of-bugs can traverse causation directly.'
Files list includes docs/ticket-model.md, docs/event-schema.md, docs/cli-reference.md — user/reference docs — but no ADR or AGENTS.md update.
T5e(c) requires: 'A novel architectural decision is captured in an ADR / AGENTS.md / design-doc update.'
findings.finding: The plan introduces a deliberate semantic design decision — `caused_by` as a distinct causal relation separate from `discovered_from` (temporal) — but lists no ADR or AGENTS.md update to capture this rationale beyond the ephemeral plan text.
findings.location: ## Approach — 'A dedicated relation (not reusing discovered_from, whose semantics are temporal not causal)'
scenarios: A future contributor adding a third relation (e.g., `triggered_by`) has no persisted record of why causal and temporal semantics were split into separate relation types, risking re-merger or duplication.
When onboarding, the rationale for the 7-relation graph model and the causal/temporal distinction is only in the plan (ephemeral), not in the living design docs.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Link Relation Design Decisions' section to docs/ticket-model.md (or a new docs/adr/007-caused-by-relation.md) that records: (1) the causal vs. temporal semantic split, (2) the policy that non-blocking/non-cycle/Jira-unmapped relations follow the discovered_from pattern, and (3) when a new relation warrants its own type vs. reuse of an existing one.

findings.finding: The plan specifies no validation error UX or AC coverage for the case where a user supplies an invalid/misspelled relation name (e.g. `caused-by`, `causedbby`) to `rebar link`. The only CLI non-happy-path tested is the self-cycle; invalid relation names are unaddressed.
scenarios: User runs `rebar link PROJ-1 PROJ-2 caused-by` (hyphen) or misspells the relation — unclear whether they get a clear validation error, a schema error, or a Python traceback
